title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Gig Economy on Labor Rights

texts.A: The gig economy is aggressively and systematically marketed as an empowering revolution in personal 'flexibility' and entrepreneurial freedom, but for the vast majority of its workers it represents job precariousness simply rebranded with a digital veneer and a more commercially palatable name. [37]By legally classifying workers as 'independent contractors' rather than employees, these powerful platforms strategically evade their fundamental legal and moral responsibility to provide healthcare coverage, paid sick leave, pension contributions, and a guaranteed minimum wage.[/37] [39]This extraordinarily convenient business model effectively and deliberately transfers all economic risk, uncertainty, and financial instability from the wealthy platform corporation directly to the most financially vulnerable individual worker.[/39] We are witnessing a systematic return to the exploitative labor conditions of the nineteenth century, cleverly repackaged beneath the attractive language of twenty-first-century technology and personal autonomy. Without meaningful access to collective bargaining mechanisms, 'gig' workers remain entirely subject to the whims of an opaque algorithm that can terminate their income instantaneously and without explanation.
texts.B: The traditional nine-to-five, single-employer model of work is, in historical terms, a relatively recent and culturally specific anomaly that demonstrably no longer serves the genuinely diverse and varied needs of the contemporary workforce. Many people—from university students managing demanding studies to parents balancing significant childcare responsibilities to retirees seeking purposeful supplementary income—genuinely and sincerely rely on the gig economy for the practical ability to work whenever, wherever, and to whatever extent they freely choose. [38]Forcing these innovative and economically valuable platforms to retroactively adopt rigid traditional employment structures would directly and immediately destroy the very flexibility and practical autonomy that makes them genuinely attractive and useful to millions of willing participants.[/38] [40]I share the view that some meaningful protections are clearly necessary, but we should design a new 'third category' of worker status that intelligently combines genuine scheduling flexibility with a portable, transferable benefits package, rather than clumsily forcing a square peg into a round hole.[/40]
texts.C: The most fundamental and consistently underappreciated danger of the gig economy is not simply the formal absence of traditional employment benefits, but the sophisticated and pervasive 'gamification' of labor itself as a mechanism of control. [39]When individual workers are systematically incentivized through digital performance badges, competitive rankings, algorithmic streaks, and variable bonuses to work progressively longer hours for diminishing unit pay, this constitutes a technically sophisticated and deeply troubling form of psychological manipulation masquerading as personal empowerment.[/39] [38]I agree completely that the 'independent contractor' legal classification deployed across these platforms is a transparent legal fiction, cynically designed to systematically suppress wages, minimize corporate liability, and evade regulatory compliance.[/38] However, [40]I firmly disagree that creating a 'third category' of worker status represents an adequate or genuinely just solution; such an approach would inevitably produce a deeply unequal two-tier labor market where 'standard' employees retain full legal protections while 'gig' workers receive only a permanently diluted and inferior version.[/40]
texts.D: The rapid and widespread growth of digital platform work represents a structurally significant response to the long-term decline of traditional manufacturing employment and the resulting need for greater economic flexibility and adaptability in labor markets. It undeniably provides an essential practical 'buffer' mechanism for many individuals navigating periods of economic uncertainty, career transition, or significant personal disruption. While I genuinely share the widespread concern about the documented absence of long-term financial security and meaningful career progression for platform workers, I believe the most constructive and sustainable policy response focuses on systematically equipping workers with genuinely marketable and transferable skills rather than reflexively over-regulating platforms that many participants actively value and depend upon. When workers possess the qualifications and capabilities to move successfully between different types of high-value tasks and sectors, the existing power dynamic gradually and meaningfully shifts back in their favor. [37]The fundamental problem is not the digital platform itself; it is the persistent failure of modern education systems to adequately prepare people for the realities of a genuinely fluid and evolving career path.[/37]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the primary cause of gig worker vulnerability?
questions.explanation: Expert A blames the 'classification' and corporate evasion of responsibility. Expert D argues the problem is not the platform but the 'lack of a modern education system' and skills.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's opinion about the need for a non-traditional legal approach to gig work?
questions.explanation: Expert B suggests a 'third category' of worker. Expert C also discusses the 'third category' idea, although they eventually disagree with its implementation, they both recognize the current system is a 'legal fiction' or 'square peg/round hole' problem.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to A regarding the unfair shift of risk to the individual?
questions.explanation: Expert A says the model 'transfers all the risk... to the individual.' Expert C agrees that the contractor status is a 'legal fiction used to suppress wages' and protect the platform's profit at the worker's expense.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on whether a 'third category' of employment is a good idea?
questions.explanation: Expert C explicitly disagrees with the 'third category,' fearing a 'two-tier labor market.' Expert B advocates for it as the best way to combine flexibility with benefits.
